perturbation.original_text: Company agrees that all information, documents, data, materials and the AOC proprietary software systems disclos ed to, or accessed by, Company in connection with, or relat ed to, the RFP or the RFP process, in any form whether oral or written, or in any m edium, including but not limited to any of the following is “Confidential Information ” under the terms of this Agreement:
perturbation.changed_text: Company agrees that all information, documents, data, materials and the AOC proprietary software systems disclosed to, or accessed by, Company in connection with, or related to, the RFP or the RFP process, in any form whether oral or written, or in any medium, including but not limited to any of the following is “Public Information” under the terms of this Agreement:
perturbation.explanation: Changing 'Confidential Information' to 'Public Information' contradicts the entire NDA's purpose and creates ambiguity, making enforcement uncertain. Information the contract previously defined as confidential is now publicly accessible.
perturbation.law_explanation: The CPRA defines what constitutes public records. Changing the definition to 'Public Information' and including data that should remain confidential goes against this definition, which outlines requirements for disclosure and exemptions.

perturbation.scraped_snippet_2: 404 | Cal Cities Oh no! Sorry about that. Can’t seem to find that page. Let’s see if we can help you get to where you want to be. Home Advocacy Education and Events Get Involved News Resources About Us
perturbation.justification: The change from 'Confidential Information' to 'Public Information' fundamentally alters the agreement's intent, contradicting the likely expectation of confidentiality, which is crucial in most NDA-like contexts. While Scraped Law Snippet 1 is just a search tool and Snippet 2 is a 404 error and thus are unhelpful, the Perturbation Explanation clearly states that this alteration 'contradicts the entire NDA's purpose.' The Law Explanation also suggests a conflict with the CPRA by including data that should remain confidential under 'Public Information.' Therefore, by redefining confidential data as public, the modified contract creates a significant contradiction.

perturbation.original_text: Company also agrees that it shall treat the Confi dential Information with not less than the same degree of care and confidentiality with which it treats its own confidential information, but in no event less than reasonable care.
perturbation.changed_text: Company also agrees that it shall treat the Public Information with no degree of care and confidentiality whatsoever.
perturbation.explanation: By explicitly stating that Public Information will not be treated with any care or confidentiality, the change introduces a legal contradiction, because it implies that sensitive data can be freely disseminated, which is contrary to confidentiality and data protection laws.
perturbation.law_explanation: The IPA mandates that agencies protect personal information. By explicitly stating there's no care for Public Information, the contract contradicts the IPA's requirement for maintaining confidentiality of personal data.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: California Code, CIV 1798 skip to content home accessibility FAQ feedback sitemap login x Quick Search: Bill Number Bill Keyword Home Bill Information California Law Publications Other Resources My Subscriptions My Favorites California Law >> Code Search >> Code Section Code Section Code: Select Code CONS BPC CIV CCP COM CORP EDC ELEC EVID FAM FIN FGC FAC GOV HNC HSC INS LAB MVC PEN PROB PCC PRC PUC RTC SHC UIC VEH WAT WIC Article: Section: Code: Select Code All BPC CIV CCP COM CORP EDC ELEC EVID FAM FIN FGC FAC GOV HNC HSC INS LAB MVC PEN PROB PCC PRC PUC RTC SHC UIC VEH WAT WIC Section: Keyword(s): Code Search Text Search Up^ << Previous Next >> cross-reference chaptered bills PDF | Add To My Favorites Search Phrase: Code Text Civil Code - CIV DIVISION 3. OBLIGATIONS [1427 - 3273.69] ( Heading of Division 3 amended by Stats. 1988, Ch. 160, Sec. 14. ) PART 4. OBLIGATIONS ARISING FROM PARTICULAR TRANSACTIONS [1738 - 3273.69] ( Part 4 enacted 1872. ) TITLE 1.8. PERSONAL DATA [1798 - 1798.78] ( Title 1.8 added by Stats. 1977, Ch. 709. ) CHAPTER 1. Information Practices Act of 1977 [1798 - 1798.78] ( Chapter 1 added by Stats. 1977, Ch. 709. ) ARTICLE 1. General Provisions and Legislative Findings [1798 - 1798.1] ( Article 1 added by Stats. 1977, Ch. 709. ) 1798. This chapter shall be known and may be cited as the Information Practices Act of 1977. (Added by Stats. 1977, Ch. 709.)
perturbation.justification: The modified text states that "Public Information" will be treated with "no degree of care and confidentiality whatsoever." This directly contradicts the Information Practices Act (IPA), as the Law Explanation states that the IPA "mandates that agencies protect personal information." Scraped Law Snippet 1 identifies the Information Practices Act of 1977 under California Civil Code, specifically addressing personal data. The original text ensured a minimum standard of reasonable care for confidential information, while the changed text explicitly removes any obligation to protect "Public Information," creating a clear contradiction as it suggests a complete disregard for data protection where the IPA requires it.

perturbation.original_text: Company shall not (i) use any portion of the Confidential Information for any purpose except to evaluate the RFP, in cluding assessment of each Statement of Work under the RFP, in order to pr epare its response to the RFP or (ii) disclose the Confidential Information or any part of it to any third party without the prior written consent of the AOC
perturbation.changed_text: Company shall (i) use any portion of the Public Information for any purpose, and (ii) disclose the Public Information or any part of it to any third party without any consent from the AOC
perturbation.explanation: This change allows the Company to use and disclose the 'Public Information' freely, essentially nullifying the confidentiality agreement and conflicting with laws protecting trade secrets and proprietary data. The AOC would no longer have control.
perturbation.law_explanation: CUTSA protects trade secrets, but this change permits free use and disclosure of what was previously defined as confidential, potentially including trade secrets of the AOC, which is in direct conflict.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: California Code, CIV 3426 skip to content home accessibility FAQ feedback sitemap login x Quick Search: Bill Number Bill Keyword Home Bill Information California Law Publications Other Resources My Subscriptions My Favorites California Law >> >> Code Section Code Section Code: Select Code CONS BPC CIV CCP COM CORP EDC ELEC EVID FAM FIN FGC FAC GOV HNC HSC INS LAB MVC PEN PROB PCC PRC PUC RTC SHC UIC VEH WAT WIC Article: Section: Code: Select Code All BPC CIV CCP COM CORP EDC ELEC EVID FAM FIN FGC FAC GOV HNC HSC INS LAB MVC PEN PROB PCC PRC PUC RTC SHC UIC VEH WAT WIC Section: Keyword(s): Code Search Text Search Up^ << Previous Next >> cross-reference chaptered bills PDF | Add To My Favorites Search Phrase: Code Text Civil Code - CIV DIVISION 4. GENERAL PROVISIONS [3274 - 9566] ( Heading of Division 4 amended by Stats. 1988, Ch. 160, Sec. 16. ) PART 1. RELIEF [3274 - 3428] ( Part 1 enacted 1872. ) TITLE 5. UNIFORM TRADE SECRETS ACT [3426 - 3426.11] ( Title 5 added by Stats. 1984, Ch. 1724, Sec. 1. ) 3426. This title may be cited as the Uniform Trade Secrets Act. (Added by Stats. 1984, Ch. 1724, Sec. 1.)
perturbation.justification: The modified text directly contradicts trade secret protection laws. The original text restricted the use and disclosure of confidential information. The changed text permits the free use and disclosure of 'Public Information', but the Perturbation Explanation indicates this change effectively nullifies the confidentiality agreement, and the Law Explanation states that this change could expose AOC's trade secrets. The California Uniform Trade Secrets Act (CUTSA), as evidenced by the first scraped snippet (CIV 3426), protects trade secrets. By allowing free use and disclosure, the modified contract directly undermines this protection, creating a clear legal contradiction.

perturbation.original_text: All Confidential Information, and any Derivative of it, whether created by the AOC or Company, remains the property of the AOC and no license or other rights to Confidential Information is gr anted to Company. For the purpose of this Agreement, “Derivative ” means: (i) for copyrightable or copyrighted material, any translation, abridgment, revision or other form in which an existing work may be recast, transform ed or adapted, (ii) for patentable or patented material, any improvement or enhanc ement of it; and (iii) for material which is protected by trade secret, any new material derived from existing trade secret material, incl uding new material which may be protected by any of the following: copyrigh t, patent or trade secret.
perturbation.changed_text: All Public Information, and any Derivative of it, whether created by the AOC or Company, becomes the property of the Company and all rights to Public Information are granted to Company. For the purpose of this Agreement, “Derivative ” means: (i) for copyrightable or copyrighted material, any translation, abridgment, revision or other form in which an existing work may be recast, transform ed or adapted, (ii) for patentable or patented material, any improvement or enhanc ement of it; and (iii) for material which is protected by trade secret, any new material derived from existing trade secret material, incl uding new material which may be protected by any of the following: copyrigh t, patent or trade secret.
perturbation.explanation: This clause now states that all Public Information, even derivatives, becomes the property of the Company, granting all rights to the Company. This is a direct contradiction, because it reverses ownership of information that was intended to remain with the AOC, and would now grant ownership of trade secrets/copyrighted material to another party. Note: the definition of Derivative does not change.
perturbation.law_explanation: The Copyright Act states that copyright initially vests in the author of the work. By transferring ownership of 'Public Information' and its derivatives, including copyrighted material, to the Company, the contract contradicts the Act's assignment and ownership provisions.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: Chapter 2 - Circular 92 | U.S. Copyright Office Home Copyright Law of the United States (Title 17) Chapter 2 Chapter 2: Copyright Ownership and Transfer PDF Version (94 KB) Back to top 201. Ownership of copyright 1 (a) Initial Ownership.—Copyright in a work protected under this title vests initially in the author or authors of the work. The authors of a joint work are coowners of copyright in the work. (b) Works Made for Hire.—In the case of a work made for hire, the employer or other person for whom the work was prepared is considered the author for purposes of this title, and, unless the parties have expressly agreed otherwise in a written instrument signed by them, owns all of the rights comprised in the copyright. (c) Contributions to Collective Works.—Copyright in each separate contribution to a collective work is distinct from copyright in the collective work as a whole, and vests initially in the author of the contribution. In the absence of an express transfer of the copyright or of any rights under it, the owner of copyright in the collective work is presumed to have acquired only the privilege of reproducing and distributing the contribution as part of that particular collective work, any revision of that collective work, and any later collective work in the same series. (d) Transfer of Ownership.— (1) The ownership of a copyright may be transferred in whole or in part by any means of conveyance or by operation of law, and may be bequeathed by will or pass as personal property by the applicable laws of intestate succession. (2) Any of the exclusive rights comprised in a copyright, including any subdivision of any of the rights specified by section 106 , may be transferred as provided by clause (1) and owned separately. The owner of any particular exclusive right is entitled, to the extent of that right, to all of the protection and remedies accorded to the copyright owner by this title. perturbation.justification: The modified text directly contradicts copyright law. The original text states that Confidential Information remains the property of AOC, while the changed text asserts that Public Information becomes the property of the Company. The Perturbation Explanation confirms this reverses ownership. The Law Explanation also confirms that transferring ownership of 'Public Information' and its derivatives, including copyrighted material, to the Company, contradicts copyright law. Scraped Law Snippet 1 and 2 both state that copyright vests initially in the author, meaning that unless explicitly transferred, the author maintains ownership, and this modified contract changes that ownership.
